coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanomaterials have gained significant attention because of their unique tunable properties, including high surface area, excellent electrical conductivity, and chemical stability. These materials are classified by dimension, including 0D carbon quantum dots, 1D nanofibers, 2D nanosheets, and 3D hierarchical nanostructures such as hollow nanocages. Hollow carbon nanocages exhibit distinct characteristics, such as interior cavities and subnanometer channels, which enhance their structural stability and electrocatalytic efficiency. Furthermore, these structures, particularly when doped with heteroatoms like nitrogen, offer promising applications in energy storage, conversion, and sensing technologies. Nitrogen doping significantly influences the electronic properties, creating additional energy levels and active catalytic sites. Doping also facilitates extreme bending of the graphene planes, which improves electrocatalytic performance by enhancing oxygen reduction reactions and increasing active site density. This paper demonstrates, through both experimental and theoretical methods, that nitrogen atoms preferentially accumulate at the edges of carbon nanocages, inducing curvature in the graphitic structure. This finding provides insight into how heteroatom doping can be leveraged to tune the structural and electrochemical properties of carbon nanomaterials for advanced applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Left: Examples of Graphitic (A) and Pyridinic (B) configurations for the nitrogen arrangement bent at different angles. Right: Examples of rolled graphitic carbon layers (C) folded at different angles.

Left Panel: Carbon K-edge XRS spectra of carbon nanotubes grown from DETA and TETA precursors (bottom) as well as different nanocubes (top). Central Panel: HR-TEM pictures of the different nanostructures. Right Panel: Nitrogen K-edge XRS spectra of carbon nanotubes grown DETA and TETA precursors (bottom) as well as different nanocubes (top).

Left: Comparison of the nanotube XRS spectra collected at the Carbon K-edge, together with a reference nanotube sample. Right: Comparison of nanocubes and nanotube XRS spectra measured at the Nitrogen K-edge.
Curve-fitted high resolution N1s XPS spectra.

Left: Comparison of experimental (dot line blue) and calculated (red solid line) XRS signals of 1,10-phenantroline measured at C K edge. Right: Experimental (dot line blue) 1,10-phenantroline and p-DOS signals: px black dot dash line, py black dash line, pz red dot dot line, total p black solid line. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Left: Comparison of experimental (dot line blue) and calculated (red solid line) XRS signals of 1,10-phenantroline measured at N K edge. Right: Experimental (dot line blue) 1,10-phenantroline and p-DOS signals: px black dot dash line, py black dash line, pz red dot dot line, total p black solid line. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Upper panel: Calculated XRS carbon K-edge spectra of graphene sheet model structures for different bending angles 0–30°. Bottom panel: Comparison of calculated data and experimental signal.
Calculated XRS spectra at the Carbon K edge obtained by rolling up the carbon sheet at 2, 5 and 10°. The experimental signal is also reported.

Upper panel: Calculated XRS spectra at the Nitrogen K edge obtained by bending at different angles (0–30° for the N-doped graphene sheet. The experimental signals are also shown. Bottom panel: Comparison of calculated data rolled up at 2, 5 and 10° compared with the experimental signals.

Right: Calculated XRS signals of pyridinic- and graphitic-like configurations upon different bending angles. The experimental data of the nanotube and nanocubes are also reported for comparison reasons. Left: corresponding layer arrangement used as input for the calculated signals. Dark blue shows the position of the scatterer. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Upper panel: Calculated p-DOS at N K-edges for both graphitic and pyridinic-like configurations upon different bending angles.
Surface elemental composition of the nanomaterials. Data are reported as mean values ± 1S (n = 3) (See also Fig. S2).
Curve fitting results of the high-resolution N1s XPS spectra. Data are reported as mean values ± 1S (n = 3) [65,66].

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanomaterials have gained significant attention because of their unique tunable properties, including high surface area, excellent electrical conductivity, and chemical stability. These materials are classified by dimension, including 0D carbon quantum dots, 1D nanofibers, 2D nanosheets, and 3D hierarchical nanostructures such as hollow nanocages. Hollow carbon nanocages exhibit distinct characteristics, such as interior cavities and subnanometer channels, which enhance their structural stability and electrocatalytic efficiency. Furthermore, these structures, particularly when doped with heteroatoms like nitrogen, offer promising applications in energy storage, conversion, and sensing technologies. Nitrogen doping significantly influences the electronic properties, creating additional energy levels and active catalytic sites. Doping also facilitates extreme bending of the graphene planes, which improves electrocatalytic performance by enhancing oxygen reduction reactions and increasing active site density. This paper demonstrates, through both experimental and theoretical methods, that nitrogen atoms preferentially accumulate at the edges of carbon nanocages, inducing curvature in the graphitic structure. This finding provides insight into how heteroatom doping can be leveraged to tune the structural and electrochemical properties of carbon nanomaterials for advanced applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to their uncommon and tunable properties [1], the interest in carbon nanomaterials has increased tremendously in recent decades. They are characterized by different structures, dimensions, high surface area, peculiar physicochemical properties, excellent electrical transport, and chemical stability [2,3]. Carbon nanomaterials are classified according to their dimension [4]: zero-dimensional carbon quantum dots [5], 1D carbon nanofibers [6], 2D carbon nanosheets [7], and 3D hierarchical nanostructures [8,9]. Structures outside of this classification include hollow carbon nanocages [10]. Typically, these nanocage structures are composed of superimposed sp2 carbon layers, forming a carbon shell with internal cavities and nanochannels, that bestow particular properties. In addition to the typical properties of carbon nanostructures, such as electronic properties and specific surface area [11,12], these nanostructures are characterized by interior cavities with sub-nanometer microchannels across the shell [3], imparting high structural stability and specific properties [2,11]. Due to the particular micro environment they can act as artificial nanoreactors and, if loaded with metals, show a void-confinement effect, which improves electrocatalytic efficiency [10]. These properties are also boosted by the abundant topological defects on their outer surface [12]. Since the hollow nanostructures are composed of conductive carbon units, they can be considered as resonant cavities. A 3D network of many of these nanocages therefore constitutes a resonant group in electromagnetic fields with unique magnetic characteristics [3]. Moreover, these 3D structures help reduce bulk density and complex permittivity [3] and form hierarchical architectures, in which interconnected, micro-, meso-, and macro-pores exist, which facilitate mass and charge transport [11]. Due to their intriguing and distinctive structural and morphological behavior, nanocages are promising materials for advanced energy storage and conversion [11–17] as well as sensors [18,19]. Their doping with heteroatoms, in particular nitrogen, leads the way to obtain interesting and promising electrocatalysts toward oxygen [20] and CO2 reduction [21], batteries [22], sensors [23], and supports for Fischer−Tropsch catalysts [24]. Tuning the bending and curvature of the graphene planes, which form the shell of these hollow nanostructures, modifies not only their optical properties but also their electronic properties, as evidenced in many theoretical studies [25–27]. The bending therefore affects their binding energies and adsorption barrier [25] with modification of their electrocatalytic properties [28]. In particular, in the literature it is evidenced that bending induces a small band gap (≈0.05 eV), which, however, is not sufficient to eliminate the electrical conductance of graphene in real device applications [25]. Electronic transmission measurements show that the conductance of bent graphene is almost exactly the same as that of flat graphene [25]. However, large bending angles above 70° will lead to a drop in conductance in the high energy regions above ≈2 eV [25]. Although from a theoretical point of view graphene can be bent above 90°, the maximum bending obtained experimentally on a single sheet of pure graphene is about 50–65° [29], which decreases further in multilayer graphene [30]. This is confirmed by transmission electron microscopy images reported in many studies, based on pure carbon nanocages, in which curved angles characterize these nanostructures[11–19]. The right angles are obtained only for nanocages doped with nitrogen [22,31], evidencing that extreme bending in graphene (above 70°) requires doping of carbon planes with nitrogen. Furthermore, nitrogen doping of carbon nanostructures affects their physicochemical properties. This is due to the combination of many factors: i) drastic changes in the energy band structure including the creation of additional energy levels [32,33], ii) the formation of very active catalytic sites [34,35] and a large amount of defects in the carbon nanostructure, and iii) the formation of several nitrogen functionalities ranging from aromatic, for example, pyridine, to aliphatic, e.g. amine, as observed by X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) [36]. However, XPS signals of N species in carbon materials allow to get information mainly at the surface [34,37] level. Moreover, in terms of the usefulness and electrocatalytic activity of these materials, the presence of this dopant and its nature is important as well as its position in the graphene plane. In fact, in the literature, it is highlighted that the presence of edges enriched in nitrogen increases diffusion kinetics [38], reversible capacity, ultrafast rate performance and good cycling stability [39], thus, it retains or recovers the spin density of pristine graphene, resulting in an increased active site density [40], and hence improving the electrocatalytic activity, as in oxygen reduction reaction, due to the ability of these active sites to favour dissociation of molecular oxygen [40]. Considering nanocages, the presence of “intrinsic” edges could be useful to impart and improve their electrocatalytic activity. Furthermore, there are some papers evidencing the importance of the presence and the nature of different defects to improve selectively the electrochemical activity toward different electrocatalytic activity [41–43]. In particular, it is evidenced that the presence of dopants, as nitrogen, induces an asymmetric redistribution of spin and/or charge of C atoms in the nearby area of the dopant [43–46], this could break the symmetry in charge/spin distribution of sp2-conjugated carbon materials, as graphene, and then increase the reactivity of these sites [43,47–50]. Moreover, it has been demonstrated that topological defects, as pentagon or heptagon instead of hexagons in the graphene plane, and edges favour heteroatoms doping of graphene plane [43]. These dopants can be accommodated in these “geometrical forms” that facilitate oxygen reactions, i.e. evolution and reduction. Moreover, the presence of these defects induces a curvature of graphene plane, positive in the case of pentagons and negative for heptagons, that displaces the hexagons “fused” with pentagon or heptagon from the plane, reorganizing pz orbitals and locally producing a new electronic structure [43]. On the contrary, vacancies and holes on the plane may help hydrogen evolution reaction and, finally, the presence of nanoparticles the CO2 and N2 reduction [43]. An experimental evidence of these concepts is reported in Ref. [41]. In this paper it has been demonstrated that the presence of nitrogen is not sufficient to assure high electrocatalytic properties toward oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), nay, in the absence of useful defects, the catalytic activity is poor. The presence of graphitic nitrogen in pentagons is necessary to boost the activity [41]. Furthermore, Atanassov et al. demonstrated that to boost ORR activity it is important the presence of pyridine-like and pyrrole-like groups [51].

Understanding the nature and structural role of heteroatoms that impart greater flexibility to graphene planes, particularly in enabling extreme bending, would be highly valuable. Specifically, identifying whether these dopant atoms are located at the edges or within the planes is crucial for tailoring their concentration and type to optimize electrocatalytic properties. Using a combined experimental and theoretical approach, in this paper we will show that nitrogen atoms accumulate at the edges of nanocubes in a bent graphitic environment thus inducing the formation of carbon nanocubes.

Nitrogen-doped carbon nanostructures, nanocubes (NC) and nanotubes (NT), were synthesized by chemical vapor deposition by using triethylenetriamine (TETA) (FlukaTM, Honeywell International Inc., Charlotte, North Carolina, USA) and diethylenetriamine (DETA) as precursors, ferrocene (Sigma-Aldrich) and Fe(NO3)3 (Sigma-Aldrich) as catalyst and, finally, MgO as substrate. MgO was prepared at room temperature by sol-gel method. Some standards were used, all of them from Sigma Aldrich: Graphite, Benzoic acid, 1,10-Phenantroline, Indole, Diamond powder, Nicotinic acid, Phenanthrene, and Ammonium Fluoride. Nanocubes were synthesized inserting an aliquot of MgO (0.3 g) into a quartz reactor and heating up to 800 °C (N2, 95 cm3 min−1, ramp 5 °C min−1). Reaching this temperature, a solution of 2.5 %wt ferrocene in TETA was added drop-wise. At the end of the dripping, the reactor was quenched in air. The amorphous part of the carbon was removed by a thermal treatment in static air at 400 °C (2 h), then MgO was lixiviated by sonication in 0.5 M H2SO4 (Sigma-Aldrich) at room temperature for 90 min. Finally, after washing and filtering (Durapore® filters, 0.45 μm, Millipore), the resulting nanocubes were dried overnight at 110 °C under nitrogen flux. By changing synthesis conditions, different samples were obtained (Table 1 ). For the synthesis of nanotubes, a similar synthetic path was used. Here, the iron-based catalyst was Fe(NO3)3 and it was not mixed with the precursor, TETA or DETA, but adsorbed onto MgO (weight ratio Fe:MgO = 1:11) before the calcination at 800 °C. In fact, Fe(NO3)3 and MgO were dispersed in water and sonicated for 1 h. After filtration, this compound was dried at 110 °C under nitrogen flux for one night, and finally, it was heated and calcined at 300 °C (ramp 5.5 °C min−1, 7.5 h, N2 flux). Therefore, during the nanotube synthesis, only the precursor, TETA or DETA, was dripped at 800 °C (Flux 2.6 cm3 h−1, 70 min). Two different nitrogen carbon nanotubes were obtained by changing precursor, tube TETA (TETA NT) and DETA (DETA NT).

ce:section.ce:section-title: X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy and high resolution

ce:section.ce:para: XPS analyses were performed on a PHI 5000 Versa Probe II Scanning XPS Microprobe spectrometer (ULVAC-PHI Inc., Kanagawa, Japan). Analyses were done with a monochromatic Al Kα source (X-ray spot 100 μm) at a power of 47.6 W. Wide scans and detailed spectra were acquired in Fixed Analyzer Transmission (FAT) mode with a pass energy of 117.40 eV and 29.35 eV, respectively. An electron gun was used for charge compensation (1.0 V 20.0 μA). Data processing was performed by using the MultiPak software v. 9.9.0.8 (ULVAC-PHI Inc., Kanagawa, Japan). The position of CC in curve-fitted high-resolution C1s XPS spectra (see SI Fig. S1) was used as reference and set at 284.5 eV. The calculated shift was used to shift all the position of peaks in curve-fitted C1s, N1s and O1s high resolution XPS spectra.

High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HR-TEM) was carried out on a HR-TEM FEI Technai F20 Field Emission Gun (FEG) microscope (acceleration potential: 200 kV). Samples were dispersed in ethanol and drop casted on Holey carbon Cu grids (300 mesh, Electron Microscopy Sciences).

All XRS data were taken using the large-solid-angle spectrometer for non-resonant inelastic X-ray scattering at beamline ID20 of the ESRF, Grenoble, France [52]. X-rays from three consecutive U26 undulators were monochromatized first by a Si(111) high-heat-load double crystal monochromator and a subsequent Si(311) channel cut post-monochromator. The monochromatic photons were then focused to a spot size of approximately 50 × 50 μm2 at the sample position. All the samples were filled into quartz glass capillaries (diameter = 2 mm) to minimize preferential orientation which could spoil the XRS data and then the choice of the region of interest (roi) for the integration of the images.

XRS spectra were collected using the inverse geometry. To measure carbon K-edge spectra, we scanned the incident energy between 9.95 and 10.02 keV in steps of 0.2 eV. Measurements of the nitrogen K-edge were performed by scanning the incident energy between 10.07 and 10.1 keV, likewise in steps of 0.2 eV. This resulted in energy loss spectra with respect to the Si(660) analyzer reflection at approximately 9.7 keV. Intensities from different analyzer crystals positioned at small scattering angles were collected and averaged over resulting in an average momentum transfer of 3.2 Å. The overall energy resolution was 0.7 eV. Data acquisition took between 20 min and several hours. The longest times were needed for the measurements at N–K edge on the NC and N-doped NT samples due to their small nitrogen concentration (6–8 %) as detected by XPS measurements (see Table 2 and SI Fig. S2).

Data were extracted from the 2D detector images after software slitting contributions from the sample environment (e.g. the quartz glass capillary) as described in [53] using the XRStools software package [54]. All edges were scanned several times, the individual scans were checked for consistency, summed up and normalized to the area between 280 and 330 eV energy loss for the C K-edge and 397–420 eV energy loss for the N K-edge, respectively.

Five samples: TETA NT, DETA NT (which are nanotubes), thick NC, thin NC, and normal NC, classified according to the synthesis procedure described above, were collected at both C and N K-edges. For comparison, five different C and N reference compounds with sp2 and sp3 electronic configurations were also measured (see SI Fig. S3 and Fig. S4).

In order to guide the interpretation of the experimental results, we performed simulations of the carbon and nitrogen K-edge of a 1,10−phenantroline molecule as well as several model structures based on manually altered graphene sheets (as discussed below) using the FDMNES software package [55]. The simulations employed both the multiple scattering theory within the muffin tin (MT) approximation and the finite difference method (FDM) scheme. The latter proved better for the calculations of the simple molecules used as standards (i.e 1,10−phenantroline and ammonium fluoride) at both C and N K-edges. The MT radii were adjusted to ensure a good overlap between the different spherical potentials. In the calculation, the Hedin−Lundqvist exchange potential was applied. The approximation of non-excited absorbing atoms was used, which, in this case, better reproduced the experimental data. The final spectra are convoluted by an arc-tangent to account for the finite core-hole lifetimes as well as by a Gaussian to account for the experimental energy resolution. The resulting spectra were area normalized in the same energy loss range as the experimental data. In addition, the projected density of states (DOS) was calculated to aid the assignment of the features found in the experimental data.

As mentioned above, we simulated N and C K-edge spectra of several model structures based on manually modified graphene sheets. Each of these sheets consisted of a total of 190 atoms. To model the local atomic carbon environment in a carbon nanotube, these sheets were either bent along an axis (Fig. 1 A and B) to mimic the edge of a nanocubes and also rolled with constant curvature (Fig. 1C with 2, 5, and 10° bending step per hexagonal ring).

The bent structures were further modified by introducing a nitrogen defect in the structure along the bending axis. Two types of nitrogen dopant configurations were created: i) a pyridinic nitrogen, i.e. a 2-fold coordinated nitrogen atom with one p electron contributing to the π system of the graphite sheet (Fig. 1 A) and ii) a graphitic nitrogen, which simply replaces a carbon atom in the graphite sheet (Fig. 1), and is therefore a tertiary nitrogen expected to act as an electron donor [56–58]. These different nitrogen configurations inside the graphite sheets are well established and usually referred to as pyridine-like, and graphite-like [56,59,60]. The term pyridine-like is derived from the C–N bonds found in the organic molecule pyridine. Both of these nitrogen configurations are characterized by a nitrogen hybridized sp2.

The left panel of Fig. 2 shows experimental carbon K-edge XRS spectra of the different carbon nanocubes and nanotubes, respectively. At the low momentum transfer utilized in the experiment, the features observed in the carbon K-edge spectrum originate dominantly from transitions of carbon 1s core electrons to unoccupied final states of p-symmetry (dipole transitions). Specifically, a sharp peak at 288.5 eV (marked by a red vertical line) is attributed to transitions from the 1s core level to the π∗ band. This peak is most prominent in the carbon nanotubes and shows slightly reduced intensity in the carbon nanocubes samples. The broad resonances above 296 eV belong to the 1s → σ∗ transitions [56,61]. The spectral weight in between 292 and 296 eV is generally assigned to free electron-like interlayer states in graphite, which has been exploited to study chemical modifications of the carbon nanotube by functionalization [56,59,62,63]. This region remains nearly unchanged when comparing the different samples.

By inspection of the left panel of Fig. 2 it is evident that the spectral region of the carbon 1s → σ∗-transition around 300 eV energy loss is likewise similar for all compounds, but shows distinct sub-structure for the nanotubes (compare features at 296 and 306 eV). The feature at 298 eV, clearly visible in the spectra of the nanotubes, is markedly weaker in the spectra of the nanocubes and becomes weaker with decreasing wall thickness. High-resolutions transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of the different samples are shown in Fig. 2 in the central panel.

The acquired N K-edge XRS spectra of the same samples are shown in the right panel of Fig. 2. All spectra display similarities with features characteristic of nitrogen K-edge spectra of N containing aromatic rings. See, as an example, the N–K spectrum of 1,10-phenantroline standard shown in Fig. S4 of the supporting information [64].

In a similar fashion as for the carbon K-edge, the features can be attributed to transitions from an initial N 1s state to antibonding π∗ and σ∗ states above the Fermi level. However, unlike the sharp feature observed in the C K-edge, the π∗ region appears less pronounced. Instead, Fig. 2 highlights the presence of two distinct peaks, marked by red vertical lines at 399 and 402 eV energy loss, respectively.

Since distinct C–N bond arrangements correspond to different binding energies, which result in different π∗ excitation energies, we infer the presence of at least two differently hybridized nitrogen in the N-doped samples. Comparison with previous studies suggests the feature at 399 eV energy loss to originate from a pyridine-like configuration, whereas the stronger second π∗-feature at approximately 402 eV can be assigned to a graphitic-like arrangement [60,62]. A direct comparison between spectra of nanotubes and nanocubes at the C K-edge and the N K-edge is shown in the left and right panels of Fig. 3 , respectively.

The two peaks visible in the C K-edge spectra at 286 and ≈293 eV energy loss, broaden slightly and lose intensity when comparing the spectrum of the nanotube reference with spectra of our thick nanocubes and DETA NT spectra. Irrespective of the thickness and shape of these nanostructures, these two peaks maintain a similar peak ratio σ∗ vs π∗. Likewise, the substructure of the σ∗ band is very similar for the spectra of the nanocubes and DETA NT samples. We ascribe the broadening of the named features to limited long range order. Since the overall shape of the C K-edge spectra is preserved, we expect very similar local structures between these samples.

The direct comparison of N K-edge data of the nanocubes and nanotubes (Fig. 3) evidences a clear reduction of these two peaks in the spectra of the nanocubes compared to the nanotubes. This loss of intensity is compensated by an increase in the σ∗-region of the nanocube spectra, implying that both, pyridine-like and graphitic like nitrogen are reduced in the nanocube samples and N adopts sites with increased sp3 character.

Up to now we have not considered the possible occurrence of graphitic nitrogen in pentagons located in graphene plane, which have a similar binding energy to pyrrolic-like nitrogen (see Table 3). According to TEM-EELS measurements, this configuration shows a clear split in the region between 400 and 405 eV [58]. This modification is not observed in our data. However, while data in the literature refer to single walled nanostructures, both NT and NC samples here investigated, are multiwalled materials. In the literature it is well pointed out that both structure and properties of carbon nanostructures depend on the atomic arrangement taking place during the organization of graphene sheets and their size [67–70], and it can be assumed that this behavior applies not only to NT but also to NC. Therefore, the lack of peak splitting can be justified considering that in multiwalled the interactions that generate it are altered.

The spectra obtained at the N K-edge can be interpreted in terms of various nitrogen environments, potentially elucidating the decrease in peak intensities at 399 and 402 eV in the nanocubes compared to the nanotubes, as well as the observed increase in the σ∗ peak at 406 eV in the nanocubes, however, the underlying cause of how nitrogen triggers the formation of cube-like nanostructures remains unresolved.

In this context, it is crucial to consider that due to the presence of a vacancy in the vicinity of the N atom, the pyridine-like N environment possesses more p-electron density. DFT calculations have shown that in the vicinity of the vacancy, a contraction of C–C distances and other structural distortions can occur that increase the total energy locally due to the breaking of the symmetry [57,62].

On the other hand, the extra electron in the graphitic-like nitrogen can destabilize the structure, resulting in a tendency to push this electron away from the plane. It may therefore be hypothesized that introduction of graphitic-like N atoms, enables bending of the sheets. Therefore, according to the synthesis protocol used here, the presence of graphitic-like N atoms in the structure could ultimately facilitate the formation of a cubic shape.

Motivated by these intriguing questions and to corroborate these hypotheses, we simulated spectra at both C and N K-edges, using model structures of pure and N-doped graphite sheets, each consisting of 190 atoms. These sheets were then bent or rolled at different angles as described in section II (see Fig. 1 right panel for stick-and-ball representations of these structures).

The model structures were used to calculate the spectra of one of the C atoms in the vicinity of the bending line or the dopant N atom. Graphitic-like and pyridine-like nitrogen arrangements were also considered in the atomic layer arrangement (see Fig. 1 right panel).

To validate the calculation procedure, we simulated the spectra of 1,10−phenanthroline at both the C and N K-edge. The results are compared to the experimental C and N K-edge spectra in the left panel of Figs. 5 and 6
, respectively. The three components of the p-density of states (p-DOS) px, py, and pz, are shown in the right panel of the same figures in comparison to the same experimental spectra shown also in the left panel.

The simulated spectra align well with the measurements at both, the N and C K-edge. All peaks as well as their relative intensities and positions are reproduced satisfactorily. Regarding the C-edge (Fig. 5), it is evident that the px and py p-DOS exhibit high similarity with high intensity in the π∗ region with a small contribution in intensity in the σ∗ region. In contrast, the pz spectrum shows its small intensity in the π∗ region. Concerning the N edge (see Fig. 6 right panel), upon examining the calculated p-DOS, it is apparent that the pz-DOS exhibits its highest intensity in the π∗ region, both at 399 and at 402 eV energy loss. On the other hand, the px and py predominantly reproduce the σ∗ region.

The results of our C K-edge calculation of the model structures consisting of graphene sheets bent by angles between 0 and 30° are reported in Fig. 7 . To align the results with the formation of the nanotube structure and to evaluate σ-π rehybridization upon diameter decrease, the rolling up of the same graphene sheet for 2, 5, and 10 degree angles has also been considered. For each calculation, we report the difference of total energy of each bent configuration calculated with respect to the unbent structure, and the corresponding Fermi energies in Table 4 . At the C–K edge, negligible variations in the π∗ peak are observed when changing the bending angle. Interestingly, as depicted in Fig. 7, an increase in the bending angle results in modifications to the sp2 hybridization. The main modification involves the px-py orbitals, while the pz, primarily responsible for the π∗ region, remains unchanged.

As what concerns the calculation of rolled structures, these are reported in Fig. 8 . Notably, there are no significant differences in the spectra simulated using bending or rolling sheets for the same angle (see Fig. 8). This suggests that local environment of the C in both arrangements is pretty much the same. Hence, similar conclusions can be drawn for both simulated structures. Remarkably, calculations based on this simple structure allow to reproduce all the features of the experimental signal (see Fig. 8). Thus, from a carbon perspective, it can be concluded that no significant locally electronic differences are expected between the nanocubes and nanotube. This observation aligns with the fact that the majority of carbon maintains sp2 hybridization consistently. Hence, regardless of the atoms in the bending line which undergoes substantial modifications, no relevant electronic changes are expected [56,58,60,71]. Examining the total energy values in Tables 4 it becomes evident that increasing the angle in the undoped material beyond 10 degrees of bending is not energetically favorable. In the nanotube, bonding primarily involves sp2 orbitals. The circular curvature induces a change in the hybridization that becomes more pronounced with decreasing diameter [72,73]. As reported in literature [72,73], three σ bonds are located slightly out of plane, leading to increased delocalization of π orbitals outside the bonding plane. Eventually, this results in an extensive π conjugation beyond the nanotube. A similar effect is observed here for the atoms situated along the bending line. However, as the bending angle exceeds 10°, this deformation has a stronger influence on sp hybridization. Concurrently, an enhanced p-electron conjugation outside the ring renders the structure unstable.

A different scenario unfolds at the N–K edge. As depicted in Fig. 9 , which considers graphitic carbon, an increase in the bending angle results in a decrease in the π∗ peak, while the σ∗ band, after a slight initial increase, also decreases. Except for the missing peak at 399 eV, the calculated signals for graphitic nitrogen reasonably match the experimental spectra. The variation in π∗ can be explained by considering the p-DOS of the nitrogen atom. In the unbent sheet, the π∗ arises from the px-py orbitals, with the pz not contributing in this region, consistent with previous calculations reported on the N K-edge [58] for a graphitic N environment. Thus, the bending alters the hybridization of the px-py orbitals making them less empty. With increasing bending, a modification occurs, and in this case, pz becomes the main contributor to π∗. Upon bending, the contribution π∗, which accounts for the component corresponding to graphitic nitrogen (at 402 eV), decreases. This variation is consistent across all bent structures that contain nitrogen. The observation suggests that nitrogen, unlike carbon, can modify its sp2 hybridization by leveraging its additional electron, which can now be close to sp3 (this agrees with the observation that the sp3 reference spectrum has a dominant σ∗ peak. (see Fig. S3 and S4).

On the other hand, the bending helps to push out of the plane the extra electrons. Consequently, these modifications, in contrast to carbon, minimize the impact of bending on the structure, ultimately improving the energy stability of the system, as reported in Tables 4 and 5 .

To fully understand the possible modifications upon bending, nitrogen in the pyridine-like configuration present in the layer must also be considered. Fig. 10 shows the comparison of the two different environments. The pyridine configuration has the peak at 399 eV. Upon bending from 10° to 30°, the peak initially increases and then decreases at the same energy value.

As evident in Fig. 11 , this peak arises from the px-py orbitals, with pz slightly involved only when the structure is bent. The intensity of σ∗ is not modified, although some rearrangement in sp2 hybridization occurs. However, the energy table shows that the lowest energy is in the unbent structure or in the 30° bent arrangement. This suggests that, because of the presence of the lone pair, the bending of the pyridin-like nitrogen makes the structure less stable unless it is out of the plane. It is worth noting that our data do not show an increase in the peak at 399 eV which would be expected to correspond either to an increase in the pyridinic amount or the presence of bending in nitrogen with the pyridinic configuration. In contrast, we have observed a decrease in the peak at 402 eV and an upturn of σ∗. This observation can be explained by the predominance of graphitic nitrogen along the bending line. Accordingly, this arrangement, in which the carbon is not bent, helps to achieve the most energetically favorable configuration. Finally, the calculations reported here suggest that nitrogen modifies its environment in the carbon sheet, helping to bend the layer, ultimately resulting in a cube shape. These observations are in accordance with literature data, since our materials are rich in nitrogen-containing pentagons and heptagons, that favour curvature. Furthermore, considering literature results, it is possible to hypothesize that the richness of these configurations and of pyridine-like groups favors an excellent activity of these materials toward ORR.

In this study, we report XRS spectra of doped nitrogen carbon nanotube and nanocubes collected at C and N K-edge respectively by using XRS spectroscopy. Finally, we have demonstrated through a comparison of experimental with theoretical calculations based on FDMNES code that nitrogen atoms preferentially accumulate at the edges of carbon nanocages. This accumulation plays a crucial role in inducing curvature in the graphitic structure by modifying the local electronic environment and introducing defects that enhance flexibility. The preferential location of nitrogen atoms at the edges of the graphene-like planes results in more pronounced bending which decreases the energy of the system and produces the nanocubes. This creates unique structural properties that are difficult to achieve in undoped carbon materials. This curvature significantly impacts the material's electronic structure. The result can impact the density of active sites and facilitating charge or mass transport. Moreover, the findings shed light on how the selective placement of heteroatoms, such as nitrogen, within the carbon framework can be strategically controlled to tailor the material's properties for specific applications. Ultimately, this research highlights the potential of heteroatom-doped carbon nanocages in advancing energy storage systems, sensors, and catalysts, offering new path to fine tune material properties for enhanced performance in a range of technological applications.
